title: Breach Notification Timelines by Regulation
content:
# Breach Notification Timelines by Regulation

## Overview

Data breach notification requirements vary significantly across regulatory frameworks, jurisdictions, and data types. Security teams must understand the applicable notification timelines, recipients, and content requirements to ensure compliance during incident response. Failure to meet notification deadlines can result in substantial penalties independent of the breach itself.

This guide consolidates breach notification requirements across major regulations and frameworks to serve as a quick reference during incident response. SOC analysts should use this alongside the organization's incident response plan to determine notification obligations immediately upon confirming a breach.

## Notification Timeline Summary

| Regulation | Timeline | Notify Who | Trigger |
|-----------|----------|-----------|--------|
| GDPR (EU) | 72 hours | Supervisory authority | Personal data breach likely to result in risk to individuals |
| GDPR (individuals) | Without undue delay | Affected individuals | High risk to rights and freedoms |
| HIPAA | 60 days | HHS, individuals, media (if >500) | Unsecured PHI breach |
| PCI-DSS | Immediately | Card brands, acquiring bank | Compromise of cardholder data |
| CCPA/CPRA | Expedient, no unreasonable delay | Individuals, CA AG (if >500) | Unencrypted PI acquired by unauthorized person |
| DFARS 252.204-7012 | 72 hours | DIBNet/DC3 | Cyber incident affecting CUI |
| SEC (public companies) | 4 business days (8-K) | SEC, public | Material cybersecurity incident |
| GLBA/FTC Safeguards | As soon as practicable, no later than 30 days | FTC | Unauthorized acquisition of unencrypted customer information affecting 500+ people |
| FERPA | Without unreasonable delay | Affected parents/students | Unauthorized disclosure of education records |
| NY DFS (23 NYCRR 500) | 72 hours | NY DFS Superintendent | Cybersecurity event with reasonable likelihood of material harm |
| NIS2 (EU) | 24 hours (early warning), 72 hours (full) | CSIRT/competent authority | Significant incident |
| DORA (EU financial) | 4 hours (initial), 72 hours (intermediate) | Competent authority | Major ICT-related incident |
| Australia (NDB) | 30 days | OAIC, affected individuals | Eligible data breach (serious harm likely) |
| Canada (PIPEDA) | As soon as feasible | Privacy Commissioner, individuals | Breach creating real risk of significant harm |
| Brazil (LGPD) | Reasonable timeframe | ANPD, data subjects | Incident that may result in risk to data subjects |

## Detailed Requirements by Regulation

### GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation)

**Supervisory Authority Notification (Article 33)**:

| Element | Requirement |
|---------|-------------|
| Timeline | Within 72 hours of becoming aware of the breach |
| Late notification | Must include reasons for delay if beyond 72 hours |
| Threshold | Unless breach is unlikely to result in risk to individuals |
| Content | Nature of breach, categories and approximate number of data subjects, DPO contact, likely consequences, measures taken/proposed |
| Method | Electronic notification to lead supervisory authority |
| Documentation | Must document all breaches regardless of notification requirement |

**Individual Notification (Article 34)**:

| Element | Requirement |
|---------|-------------|
| Timeline | Without undue delay |
| Threshold | Breach likely to result in high risk to rights and freedoms |
| Content | Clear, plain language description of breach, DPO contact, likely consequences, measures taken/proposed |
| Exemptions | Data was encrypted/unintelligible, subsequent measures eliminated high risk, disproportionate effort (use public communication instead) |

**Key Considerations**:
- "Becoming aware" starts when the controller has reasonable certainty a breach has occurred
- Phased notification is acceptable if full details are not available within 72 hours
- Data processors must notify data controllers "without undue delay" after becoming aware
- Document risk assessment rationale if deciding NOT to notify

### HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act)

**Breach Notification Rule (45 CFR 164.400-414)**:

| Element | Requirement |
|---------|-------------|
| Timeline | Without unreasonable delay, no later than 60 calendar days from discovery |
| Discovery date | Date breach is known or should have been known with reasonable diligence |
| Individual notification | Written notice by first-class mail or email (if agreed) |
| HHS notification | Within 60 days if 500+ individuals; annual log submission if <500 |
| Media notification | Required if 500+ residents of a single state/jurisdiction |
| Content | Description of breach, types of information involved, steps individuals should take, what the entity is doing, contact information |

**Breach Risk Assessment (four-factor test)**:

1. Nature and extent of PHI involved (types of identifiers, likelihood of re-identification)
2. Unauthorized person who used the PHI or to whom disclosure was made
3. Whether PHI was actually acquired or viewed
4. Extent to which risk has been mitigated

**Safe Harbor**: No notification required if PHI was encrypted per NIST guidelines and the encryption key was not compromised, OR if PHI was destroyed and cannot be reconstructed.

### PCI-DSS

**Card Brand Notification Requirements**:

| Card Brand | Timeline | Contact | Additional Requirements |
|-----------|----------|---------|------------------------|
| Visa | Immediately upon discovery | Visa Fraud Control | PFI investigation required |
| Mastercard | Immediately, within 24 hours | Mastercard Security | Account Data Compromise (ADC) report |
| American Express | Immediately | Enterprise Incident Response Program | |
| Discover | Immediately upon discovery | Discover Fraud Prevention | |

**PCI Forensic Investigation (PFI)**:
- Required for Level 1 and Level 2 merchants after a confirmed breach
- Must use a PCI-approved PFI
- Preliminary report within 5 business days of engagement
- Final report within 10 business days of investigation completion

### SEC Cybersecurity Disclosure Rules

**Form 8-K (Item 1.05) - Material Cybersecurity Incidents**:

| Element | Requirement |
|---------|-------------|
| Timeline | Within 4 business days of determining incident is material |
| Content | Nature, scope, timing; material impact or reasonably likely material impact |
| Materiality standard | Would a reasonable investor consider it important in making an investment decision |
| Delay provision | AG can request 30-60 day delay if disclosure poses substantial national security or public safety risk |
| Amendments | Must amend 8-K if material information was not available at initial filing |

**Form 10-K (Item 1C) - Annual Cybersecurity Disclosure**:
- Describe processes for assessing, identifying, and managing material cybersecurity risks
- Board oversight of cybersecurity risk
- Management role in assessing and managing cybersecurity risk

### DFARS 252.204-7012 (Defense Contractors)

| Element | Requirement |
|---------|-------------|
| Timeline | Within 72 hours of discovery |
| Reporting portal | DIBNet (https://dibnet.dod.mil) |
| Scope | Any cyber incident on covered contractor information systems |
| Required information | Company info, incident details, CUI affected, malicious software (if found), forensic images |
| Evidence preservation | 90 days minimum |
| Cooperation | Must cooperate with DoD damage assessment |
| Subcontractor flow-down | Must include requirement in subcontracts involving CUI |

### State Breach Notification Laws (US)

All 50 US states have breach notification laws. Key variations:

| State | Timeline | Notable Requirements |
|-------|----------|---------------------|
| California | Expedient, without unreasonable delay | AG notification if >500 residents; specific notice format required |
| New York | Expedient, without unreasonable delay | NY AG, DFS, Division of State Police notification; 23 NYCRR 500 has 72-hour requirement |
| Texas | Without unreasonable delay, within 60 days | AG notification if >250 residents |
| Florida | Within 30 days | AG notification if >500 residents; may extend to 45 days with good cause |
| Massachusetts | As soon as practicable | AG and Office of Consumer Affairs notification; free credit monitoring for SSN breaches |
| Illinois | Without unreasonable delay | AG notification; BIPA has separate biometric data requirements |
| Virginia | Without unreasonable delay | AG and affected individuals notification |
| Colorado | Within 30 days | AG notification if >500 residents |
| Oregon | Within 45 days | AG notification if >250 residents |
| Connecticut | Within 60 days | AG notification |

## Incident Response Integration

### Notification Decision Tree

```
Security incident confirmed
  |
  +-- Does it involve personal data / PI / PHI / CUI / cardholder data?
  |   |
  |   +-- NO --> Standard incident handling, no notification required
  |   +-- YES --> Continue assessment
  |
  +-- Identify all applicable regulations based on:
  |   - Data type (PI, PHI, CUI, cardholder data)
  |   - Data subject location (state, country)
  |   - Industry (healthcare, financial, defense, public company)
  |   - Contractual obligations
  |
  +-- For each applicable regulation:
  |   |
  |   +-- Does a safe harbor apply? (encryption, no actual access)
  |   |   |
  |   |   +-- YES --> Document rationale, retain evidence
  |   |   +-- NO --> Notification likely required
  |   |
  |   +-- Determine notification timeline (shortest applicable)
  |   +-- Identify notification recipients
  |   +-- Prepare notification content per requirements
  |
  +-- Escalate to Legal within 24 hours for notification determination
  +-- Begin drafting notifications for all applicable regulations
  +-- Track all notification deadlines in incident case management
```

### Notification Preparation Checklist

For each regulation requiring notification:

- [ ] Confirm the data types involved and number of affected individuals
- [ ] Identify the specific notification deadline and calculate due date
- [ ] Determine notification recipients (regulator, individuals, media, law enforcement)
- [ ] Draft notification content meeting the regulation's content requirements
- [ ] Obtain legal review and approval of notification content
- [ ] Prepare individual notification method (mail, email, substitute notice)
- [ ] Submit regulatory notifications through required channels
- [ ] Document all notifications sent (date, method, recipient, content)
- [ ] Establish contact center or FAQ page for individual inquiries
- [ ] Arrange credit monitoring or identity protection services if required

### Evidence Preservation

Regardless of notification requirements, preserve:

| Evidence Type | Minimum Retention | Notes |
|--------------|-------------------|-------|
| Forensic images | 90 days (DFARS); varies by regulation | Retain longer if litigation anticipated |
| Log data | Duration of investigation + 1 year | Include all relevant log sources |
| Network captures | Duration of investigation | Full packet captures if available |
| Malware samples | Duration of investigation + 1 year | Isolate in secure malware repository |
| Communication records | Duration of investigation + 3 years | All internal and external communications |
| Notification records | 6 years (GDPR); varies by regulation | Proof of notification sent and received |

## Common Notification Failures

1. **Missing the 72-hour window** - Investigation takes too long before notification. Begin drafting notifications in parallel with investigation; phased notification is acceptable.

2. **Failing to identify all applicable regulations** - Only notifying under one framework when multiple apply. Maintain a regulatory applicability matrix and review during every incident.

3. **Incomplete notification content** - Missing required elements in the notification. Use regulation-specific templates pre-approved by legal.

4. **Not preserving evidence of notification** - Cannot prove notification was sent on time. Document all notifications in the incident case file with timestamps and delivery confirmation.

5. **Ignoring contractual notification obligations** - Customer contracts may have stricter timelines than regulatory requirements. Review customer contracts during scoping.

6. **Delayed individual notification** - Regulatory notification sent but individual notification delayed. Plan individual notification logistics early (printing, mailing, contact center).

## References

- GDPR Articles 33-34: https://gdpr-info.eu/
- HIPAA Breach Notification Rule: 45 CFR 164.400-414
- SEC Final Rule on Cybersecurity Disclosure: https://www.sec.gov/rules/final/2023/33-11216.pdf
- DFARS 252.204-7012: https://www.acquisition.gov/dfars
- NCSL State Breach Notification Laws: https://www.ncsl.org/technology-and-communication/security-breach-notification-laws
- PCI-DSS v4.0 Incident Response Requirements
- FTC Health Breach Notification Rule: 16 CFR Part 318
